results.possibleIdentity: Shobha Singh was a research physicist at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) who was active during the 1960s and 1970s. He earned his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees from Delhi University in 1949 and 1951, followed by a Ph.D. in physics from Johns Hopkins University in 1957. After working at the National Physical Laboratory and the Bhabha Atomic Research Center in India, and the National Research Council of Canada, he joined the technical staff at Bell Labs in 1964. His research expertise included nonlinear optics, quantum electronics, electro-optics, and solid-state spectroscopy, with a specific focus on lasers and fluorescence. In 1965, he co-authored significant research on the laser generation of excitons and fluorescence in anthracene crystals. Singh was a resident of Summit, New Jersey, and was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that connected artists with engineers and scientists for collaborative projects. He was elected a Fellow of the Optical Society of America in 1981 and held over 20 patents related to optical devices and communication technologies.
